title: The practice of moving slowly when everything in you wants to rush
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

The body knows something your mind forgot 

 I was sitting on my couch last Tuesday, staring at my laptop, and my leg was bouncing. Not a gentle tap. A fu...
htmlContent:
The body knows something your mind forgot

I was sitting on my couch last Tuesday, staring at my laptop, and my leg was bouncing. Not a gentle tap. A full tremor. My jaw was clenched. My shoulders were up near my ears. And I wasn't even doing anything stressful. I was just thinking about replying to an email. A normal email. About a deadline that's still two weeks away.

But my body was already in the race. Heart rate up. Breath shallow. Muscles braced for impact. And here's the thing: I didn't even notice until about ten minutes in. That's how automatic the rush is for me. For you too, probably. We've been trained to move fast, think fast, fix fast. Speed feels safe. Slowing down feels like falling behind. Like dying.

I've been working with trauma survivors for years now, and I keep coming back to this one stubborn truth: the nervous system doesn't know the difference between a real threat and a perceived one. It just knows speed. When you've lived through complex trauma, your system learns that danger is everywhere and that the only way out is to move faster than it can catch you. So you rush through your morning coffee. You rush through conversations. You rush through your own breath. You rush through healing itself.

But recovery doesn't work that way. It can't. Your nervous system needs to feel safe enough to slow down before it can actually process anything. And that's the cruel paradox. The very thing you need to do to heal is the thing your body will fight hardest against.

Think about it. When was the last time you sat still for ten minutes without checking your phone, without planning what to say next, without mentally running through your to-do list? For most of us, it's been years. Decades maybe. And that's not a personal failing. It's a survival adaptation. Your system learned that stillness equals vulnerability. That slowing down means getting caught.

So I'm not going to tell you to just "slow down" like it's a simple choice. It's not. It's a renegotiation with your own biology. A conversation with the part of you that's convinced speed is the only thing keeping you alive.

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

Why your nervous system treats the brakes like a threat

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about something called "neuroception." It's the way your nervous system scans for safety and danger without your conscious input. And for people with complex trauma histories, that scanner is calibrated to detect threats everywhere. A raised voice. A sudden movement. A quiet room. Even a pause in conversation can trigger a full system alarm.

So when you try to slow down, your nervous system might interpret that as a sign that you're letting your guard down. That you're about to be blindsided. And it will do everything in its power to get you moving again. Racing thoughts. Restlessness. A sudden urge to clean the kitchen. That familiar feeling of "I should be doing something productive right now."

Wild, right? The very practice that could help you regulate is the one your system flags as dangerous.

I remember the first time I tried to do a slow body scan. I was lying on my yoga mat, and my therapist told me to just notice my breath. I lasted maybe thirty seconds before I sat up, heart pounding, convinced I was wasting time. That I should be working on something. Anything. The discomfort was so physical it felt like my skin was crawling.

And here's what I've learned since then: that discomfort isn't a sign that you're doing something wrong. It's a sign that you're touching something real. Your system is saying, "Hey, this doesn't feel safe. Can we please go back to the familiar chaos?" And the practice isn't to force yourself to stay still. It's to notice the urge to run and not follow it. Just for a few seconds. Then maybe a few more.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about pendulation. Moving between activation and settling, slowly, gently, like a wave. You don't go from full speed to full rest in one jump. You touch the edge of slowing down, then you let yourself move again. Then you touch it again. Each time, you're teaching your nervous system that slowing down won't kill you. That you can survive the stillness.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and the inner critic gives language to what so many of us experience but can't name.

The rush is a story your body learned to tell

I used to think my urgency was a sign of ambition. That I was just driven. Motivated. A go-getter. And maybe that was true in some ways. But underneath it, there was something else. A deep, quiet terror that if I stopped moving, I would disappear. That my worth was tied to my output. That rest was something you earned after you'd done enough, and enough was never enough.

That's the story. And it's not your fault that you learned it. It was taught to you by a world that values productivity over presence. By caregivers who couldn't hold still themselves. By a culture that treats burnout as a badge of honor. By a nervous system that learned early on that slowing down meant getting hurt.

But here's the thing about stories: they can be rewritten. Not by force. Not by willpower. But by small, repeated experiences that contradict the old narrative. Every time you choose to take one slow breath instead of rushing to the next task, you're telling your body a new story. "We can pause. We can survive this. Nothing bad happens when we slow down."

It won't feel true at first. It will feel like lying. But your nervous system learns through repetition, not through logic. You have to show it, again and again, that stillness is safe. That you can return to movement whenever you need to. That slowing down isn't a trap. It's a choice.

I want to pause here and check in with you. How's your body doing right now? Not your mind - your body. Is there tension somewhere? A heaviness? A restlessness? Whatever you find, it's information. It's your nervous system responding to what you're reading. And that response is valid.

One thing I've learned in my own recovery is that reading about trauma can be activating. Your body doesn't always know the difference between remembering something and experiencing it. So if you need to take a break, take a break. Put your feet on the floor. Look around the room. Name five things you can see. This isn't avoidance. This is regulation. There's a difference.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry. It shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you," which changes everything about how we approach healing.

What slowing down actually looks like in practice

Let me be real with you. I'm not great at this. I still catch myself rushing through meals, rushing through conversations, rushing through my own thoughts. But I've learned a few things that actually help. Not because they're perfect, but because they're possible. Even for someone whose default setting is panic speed.

First, you don't have to slow down all the way. You just have to slow down a little. If your normal pace is a 10, try an 8. If you usually eat breakfast in three minutes, try four. If you normally reply to emails immediately, try waiting sixty seconds. The goal isn't to become a monk. It's to create a tiny gap between stimulus and response. A crack where choice can enter.

Second, notice what happens when you try to slow down. Stay with me here. Don't judge it. Just observe. Does your chest tighten? Do your thoughts get louder? Do you feel a surge of irritation? That's your nervous system doing its job. It's trying to protect you. Thank it. And then gently, without forcing, see if you can stay with the discomfort for just a few more seconds. Not to conquer it. Just to be with it.

Third, use your body. Your mind won't slow down on command. But your body can. And when your body slows down, your nervous system follows. Put your hand on your chest. Feel your heartbeat. Breathe out longer than you breathe in. Let your shoulders drop. Let your jaw unclench. These aren't spiritual practices. They're physiological interventions. They work because your body doesn't know the difference between a real threat and a perceived one, but it also doesn't know the difference between real safety and a practiced one.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It helps you understand the different voices inside you, including the one that's always telling you to go faster.

The slow return to yourself

I don't have a neat conclusion for you. I wish I did. I wish I could tell you that if you just practice slowing down for thirty days, you'll be cured. But that's not how complex trauma works. It's not a linear path. You'll have days where you can sit still for twenty minutes and feel peaceful. And you'll have days where you can't sit still for twenty seconds without wanting to crawl out of your skin. Both are okay. Both are part of the process.

What I can tell you is this: the rush you feel, the urgency, the constant need to move and do and fix and achieve, it's not who you are. It's a survival strategy. A very old, very loyal one. And it deserves your compassion, not your contempt. The part of you that wants to rush isn't your enemy. It's a protector. It's been keeping you safe for a long time. Maybe too long. Maybe past its usefulness. But it doesn't know that yet. It needs to be shown, gently, patiently, that you're okay now. That you can handle the stillness. That you can survive being present.

So here's my invitation to you. Not a prescription. Not a directive. Just an invitation. The next time you notice yourself rushing, pause for one breath. Just one. Feel the air move in and out. Notice that you're still here. Notice that nothing bad happened in that second. Then do whatever you were going to do. But maybe do it a little slower. And see what that feels like.

You don't have to change everything today. You just have to start. And starting is enough.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz
